author: @guytech7310
text:
Power systems are centralized to address cost, complexity & logistics. No way are intermittent power systems like solar & wind practical for urban areas: First there isn't enough space to supply power for a high rise apartment or office building. Nor will they be cost effective as just a solar & battery storage is well beyond the cost of of median household income. Systems without batteries are useless and the grid can only support about 7% of intermittent power sources before coming unstable. Another issue with a large number of individual grid tie inverters is phase noise that destabilizes the 50\60hz since wave power. Basically inverters aren't able to track the phase like  rotary mechanical generators can do. 

Over call when the cost of fossil fuels become too expensive civilization will collapse to pre-industrial levels. In Europe this is already happening now that it lost access to Russia energy. All of the industrial companies in Europe are either going to move product out of europe or go bankrupt. Europe will likely be joining South Africa with a collapsing economy and permanent rolling blackouts. Unemployment will probably rise to 60% in Europe over the next few years. Europe only chance is if fully apologizes to Russia and pays Russia reparations for the damages it has caused, which I doubt will happen anytime soon.

Also consider than Global Oil production peaked in 2018 & is now in permanent decline. The only two nations that had any significant production increases in the past 20+ years was Russia & the US. The US was able to temporarily increase production by tapping Shale Oil, however that growth is finished & will enter terminal decline around 2027-2029. The US has been rapidly draining its strategy Oil reserves in order to prevent Oil prices from soaring, but the US SPR will be tapped out later this year (2023). The only way Oil prices will not go ballistic is because of demand destruction as the global economy falls into another deep recession.

Over all I expect the second dark ages of western civilization to begin in the 2030-2040s as Oil production collapses taking with it the economy. Already Western nations are deep in debt and don't have the funds to pay for promised pensions & entitlements. Unless your already retired you can forget about retirement, and if you are recently retired, plan to return to work as your pension\entitlement\savings won't be enough. This is because gov't will turn to printing money to keep the gov't building doors open and to delay riots. 

The best recommendation I have is to forget looking for some miracle tech solution, but to build a homestead which you can be self-reliant, especially with food. Already Europe is facing food shortages & rationing, This will not get better, and only continue to deteriorate.

author: @JA-ti3wd
text:
I'm sorry to say that your statements on nuclear power are not correct. Japan is doubling down on nuclear power not abandoning it. Given their experience they are strengthening their defences at stations that need it and have shutdown the old ones - just as the UK shutdown the Magnox stations. The AGRs in this country and the PWR at Sizewell are still active so nuclear will likely be contributing to our energy needs into the 2030s even if you ignore France and the new units being built in Somerset.

The idea that 1 problem causes cities to be abandoned gives an inaccurate message. If you're able to visit an operating station in the UK you'll find back up systems in parallel to the emergency systems which are separate to multiple 'normal' systems for the crucial safety functions. 1 error might take out a pump or generator or something but there will probably be another 2 units in the 'normal' system plus 2 complete other systems to do the same job if that job is important. It's part of the reason why nuclear is so expensive.

author: @chrisbaker3066

I have lived off- grid since 1990. In replacement and installation costs alone, I have spent around $450 AUD per year on power. This includes a small amount of petrol for a generator for large power tools and to pump water to a header tank each week. The downside is that I have had to alter the way I live, lower my expectations and be flexible when the sun is not visible on the few occasions that this occurs. 
My solar system is only 1.3 kw so that is very small  and more investment would ensure a more constant supply. The UP side is that I have lower living costs in an era when gas and electricity prices are unpredictable and soaring and I value the sense of independence and freedom from a system that is there to profit off us and not to supply a service.

author: @sinenomine9093
text:
I'm a farmer with solar panels on the barn, an electric car, and net metering.  Net metering means I give the power company all my excess power when the sun shines and they give me back power when the sun's not shining and I need it.  After four years, I'm still ahead, I've given them marginally more electricity than I've used in total.  It's not perfect but their peak electricity demand is mid-day in mid-summer, and my peak demand is late fall & winter to dry crops. We balance quite nicely. We're fortunate here to have a high proportion of hydro and a good amount of nuclear, in addition, we can buy hydro from Quebec.
  The other great thing is, in a pinch, I can charge my electric car direct from my panel so if things get really tight I still have nominal transportation without the need for gasoline.  Electricity for my car cost the equivalent of 55 cents per gallon (3.89 L) compared to $3.50 per gallon currently.
  Also, my electricity cost is amortized & fixed at 5 cents (US) per kWh for the next 25 years.  Locally, electricity costs 12-14 cents per kWh.

author: @frankkroondijk586
text: One important elephant in the room when it comes to centralized energy production and manufacturing in general is the vulnerability by terrorism, hackers, corruption, etc. You only have to see what Russia is doing to the Ukraine right now, bombing energy plants. Democratizing energy and manufacturing is very important to focus on. One of the hurdles I ran into working in the field of local smart grids was the costs involved versus the price of electricity. A big challenge, and one that brought concept down, was that the old meters in houses are working for decades while new electronics, required for smart grids, has to be replaced every few years. This combined with the relatively low price of centralized electricity made it an impossible business case back then (2008). Then there is the hurdle of legislation, for e.g. in the NL it is not allowed to create a parallel network, so this makes it impossible to buy and sell energy from your neighbours. A connection to the grid is even mandatory in NL, also in Portugal I found out recently. In new developments I ran into this rule, so even if a house is fully self sustainable, it is mandatory to connect power, water and sewage (and bearing all the costs for all the architects, engineers, construction workers, materials, etc etc.) Laws, with all their good intentions, are updated way too slow in this fast evolving world.

author: @chaorrottai
text:
You know you might call me a cook for saying it but I am firmly of the opinion that if a coefficient of performance can be achieved for concentrating thermal kinetic energy, in other words the refrigeration cycle or the carnot cycle, then I am a firm believer that there will eventually be discovered a process for concentrating electrical energy with a coefficient of performance.

Coefficients of performance are clearly a scientific reality considering that heat pumps exist.

Because we generally do not go for heat as a primary product that is the only reason in my opinion that we do not consider heat pumps to be free energy devices.

If you were to spend a hundred joules of energy spinning the pump so that you could aggregate heat to fuel and endothermic chemical process then you would give more energy to that endothermic chemical process then the energy that it took to spin the heat pump.

Just because the carnot cycle cannot be reversed to get mechanical energy at an efficiency rate that makes the coefficient of performance from that same carnot cycle device worth it does not make the coefficient of performance disappear.

Just because you can't plainly pull the kinetic energy back out that you originally put in when you allow the temperature difference to renormalize through the machine does not mean that the coefficient of performance does not matter.

One day we will figure out how to do this with electricity.

author: @russelldold4827
text:
I agree with your general argument about decentralised generation and alternative supplementation.
I do, however, question two nuclear examples commonly held up as "disasters", namely 3-Mile Island and Fukushima.
1. As far as I am aware, no release of nuclear material occurred at 3-Mile Island. The actual disaster was in the mismanagement of information and emergency procedures which resulted in public panic.
2. Fukushima's reactor structure withstood an estimated force orders of magnitude greater than the design specification. The failure lay in the damage to the external cooling systems which led to the forced closure. Again, while Fukushima had to close, this closure was successfully done without significant release.
I prefer to consider these two events as severe tests, from which the nuclear industry has drawn valuable lessons.
I used to be a proponent of nuclear energy in my own country, South Africa, but recent events in our coal-fired generation system show that the   rigid discipline and adherence to safe operating procedures necessary for nuclear generating plants cannot be relied upon.

currently the other issue with power is distribution, at the moment to get the increase required to charge all those EV's would mean upgrading a lot of the existing distribution infrastructure, more substations, bigger cables in the ground.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: Local production of electricity also allows users of DC power. DC appliances are always more efficient than AC appliances. An average 5 kw 24 volt (real voltage around 27 volts) uses 2  amp with no load. That means 54 watt and it does nothing. Then X by 24th hours means 1296 watts in total per day just to idle run the inverter.  That is double what an average house uses in LED lighting per day or double what a top loading DC 24 volt fridge uses in a 24 hour period. And that is not even including the losses of converting 27 volt DC to 220 volt mains. Imagine a 300 watt ac fridge then needs an extra 30 watts in conversion loss x 5 hours results in another 150watt  per day extra just to use a mains ac fridge. And then all the other ac appliances. Really DC is the way to go especially if using decentralised power generation. Less power generation needed, less battery demands, etc all round a better deal.

replies.author: @davidsoulsby1102
replies.text: It's only cheaper if you generate in DC from the start and run only DC applications over moderately small distances (or the opposite, huge distances with few points of isolation like transmediterainian routes) . We had this discussion a hundred years ago, DC needs bigger equipment, physical switches etc. If you have lots of equipment, that carries a huge cost. AC is much smaller, less cost, less maintenance.....that's why the world uses AC on national scales.  It is also safer due to the 0v point in the wave being easy to break compared to the constant voltage of DC.

replies.author: @bartronicsecurity
replies.text: @davidsoulsby1102 Short distances 50 to around 100 meters max. Ideally less. But remember 12 volt or 24 volt LED lights per room don't use more than an amp or 2 so you can use the same wiring and switches that are already being used for mains. In fact 24 volt fridges use 5 or 6 amp so not much more current. A 12 volt TV uses 2 or 3 amp. In reality DC does not use a great deal more current . They tend to be more efficient meaning total overall less power. Remember ac powered products don't need efficiency since there is plenty power because of the high voltage and the fact that there is a massive power source feeding that plug.

replies.author: @Maxjoker98
replies.text:
​@bartronicsecurity I think you have some misconceptions there. It's simply not true that AC is less efficient than DC for appliances. In general, you want to avoid conversion losses. That means if a device is supplied AC, but requires DC you loose a little bit of power while converting(and vice-versa). One of the main reasons AC is used would be that almost all power generation is inherently AC(no DC generators), and devices that turn power into some form of rotation are also almost always AC(motors), or don't care(heating). That includes most kitchen appliances, including fridges(the compressor is probably an AC motor), toasters(AC/DC doesn't matter), electric ovens(AC/DC doesn't matter), blenders(AC motor).
Also keep in mind that voltage regulation is also easy when you're dealing with AC. 
Computers/TV's/everything with transistors inside it are possibly more efficient from DC, but keep in mind that you still need to provide different DC voltages, so you'll still need power conversion at multiple places.

I also don't think that it's true that AC powered products don't need to care about efficiency. They obviously optimize for power, because people don't want to pay for more KWh.  
And yes, the difference in current is huge when you're comparing AC mains voltage(110V-240V depending on country) to simple DC 24V systems. Yes, you can run a LED strip of DC, but I don't think it's realistic to draw 2KW for an oven over 24V DC: That's more than 80A, and the equipment for switching 80A is way larger than just a little over 8A for a 230V system.

replies.author: @bartronicsecurity
replies.text: @Maxjoker98 I know where you are coming from.  Remember I am an electronic engineer and have been working with energy for 44 years. I have designed and manufactured solar chargers and controllers and inverters in my factory at one stage for over 10 years at one stage. I have tested and measured the best competing products on the market. I have either been to or installed some of dozens of sites using full mains AC products with inverters and all and also full DC only sites and time and again the DC sites win in cost, maintainance and workability. Take a fridge 300watt motor verse a DC fridge which uses 50watt. The AC fridge is front loading because people want convience but the top loading DC smart people know the real energy difference and use top loading for energy. Yes top loading fridges do exist but generally have a lower efficiency compressor and poorer insulation as doing a good job as per most DC versions costs extra money. Yes I know most DC items may cost more than AC items but in the long run total savings is the reason for going DC.  An AC 36inch TV uses 73 watts (I measured myself) verse DC of 27watts (I measured myself ) both same brand same features just different working voltages. Go out and measure these kind of things for yourself. Look at this with fairness on both sides and you will be surprised. For many years I believed ac was the best for off grid. Now I know it is not. I have spread sheets and real results from all my years of dealing in energy. The solar panel manufacturers, battery manufacturers and inverter manufacturers love people like you.  Sorry don't mean to be horrible just truthful. I've seen too many real live sites and now know the truth.
